does: This paper introduces SLIM, a method that interprets and steers large language model molecular editors through sparse latent feature decomposition. The approach uses a Sparse Autoencoder with learnable importance gates to map dense hidden states into interpretable, property-aligned sparse features. During inference, steering in this sparse feature space selectively activates dimensions relevant to target molecular properties, improving the success rate of property-directed edits without modifying the underlying model parameters. The method is evaluated on the MolEditRL benchmark across four model architectures and eight molecular properties, achieving improvements of up to 42.4 percentage points over baseline approaches.
connects: This work builds on established research in model steering and interpretability for language models, extending techniques that manipulate internal representations to improve specific capabilities. It combines sparse autoencoders—increasingly used for mechanistic interpretability of neural networks—with property-conditional editing, responding to limitations in prior molecular editing approaches where property-relevant information remains implicitly distributed across dense hidden states. The framework also draws from work on latent space manipulation for controlled text generation and chemical property prediction, adapting these ideas to the specific challenge of directed molecular transformation where edits frequently fail to improve target properties.
enables: This approach opens new directions for controlling generative models through interpretable latent steering, applicable beyond molecular editing to other domains requiring property-directed generation. The sparse feature decomposition enables both better empirical control and mechanistic understanding of what linguistic and chemical reasoning patterns the model uses for editing decisions. Future work could extend this framework to multi-property optimization, real-time feature attribution during generation, and discovering causal relationships between sparse features and editing outcomes. The interpretability benefits also support development of more reliable molecular design pipelines where practitioners understand model behavior before deployment.
